one_line_takeaway: A broad dual-use survey arguing generative AI lowers the barrier to malware and phishing (with a ChatGPT-API ransomware PoC and jailbreak/semantic-manipulation walkthroughs), plus a loosely attached long-term AGI-risk section; persuasive as a landscape overview but empirically thin, with its headline numbers all cited from third parties rather than measured.
key_finding: GenAI (chiefly LLMs like ChatGPT) materially lowers the barrier to entry for cybercrime, and vendor safety filters are bypassable through prompt reframing and jailbreaking; the same capabilities that strengthen defense (threat detection, prediction, automation) also arm attackers, making it a genuine dual-use technology. The authors argue current safeguards (e.g. OpenAI's moderation endpoint covering violence, self-harm, hate, and sexual content) are insufficient against semantic manipulation, and that specialized uncensored models (WormGPT, FraudGPT, HackerGPT) are already commercialized. Longer term they contend AGI/ASI risks warrant preemptive controls such as responsible scaling policies and secure model-weight storage.
core_contribution: A survey/position paper (Duke Pratt School of Engineering with Coalfire and Safebreach) mapping the dual-use of generative AI in cybersecurity. Its own demonstrative contribution is a qualitative walkthrough of how ChatGPT-3.5's safety guardrails can be circumvented via (1) 'semantic manipulation' (reframing a malicious prompt's context while preserving its purpose) and (2) 'jailbreaking' (e.g. the DAN prompt), plus a proof-of-concept Python ransomware-generation script that drives the ChatGPT API with a recursive error-feedback loop that resubmits runtime errors for correction. The paper also catalogs 'blackhat' LLMs (WormGPT, FraudGPT, HackerGPT). Its second half pivots to long-term AI risk: AGI timelines, the alignment/reward-hacking problem, and two proposed frontier-lab controls (responsible scaling policies and secure storage of model weights). NOTE: nearly all headline statistics are cited from third-party reports, not measured by the authors.
threat_model: Adversaries ranging from low-skill 'script kiddies' to sophisticated threat actors leverage GenAI to lower the skill and time barrier to offensive operations: generating malware (incl. polymorphic/self-evolving/metamorphic variants that evade signature-based detection), crafting hyper-personalized phishing and business-email-compromise, producing deepfakes/synthetic media for disinformation, and exploiting uncensored blackhat LLMs. A secondary consequence is degraded attribution (harder to identify perpetrators). High-risk sectors named: financial services, healthcare, critical infrastructure, and government. The long-term section additionally frames frontier AI labs and their model weights as an attack surface and misalignment as a systemic risk.
limitations: This is a position/survey paper with no rigorous empirical evaluation of its central claims. The eye-catching figures (80% phishing click-through, 1,265% rise in phishing, 24% of breaches in financial services) are all borrowed from third-party sources (Black Hat 2023 talk, industry reports, ITRC 2023 Data Breach Report), not produced or validated by the authors. The ransomware PoC reports no measured success rate and is acknowledged to be trivially detectable by sandboxing and static analysis. The paper's three tables are illustrative (AI milestones, AGI-timeline opinions, reward-hacking examples) rather than results tables, so there are no benchmark numbers of the authors' own. The two halves (near-term offensive infosec vs long-term AGI alignment) are only loosely connected, and sourcing leans heavily on vendor/marketing material (CyberArk, Tripwire).

quantitative_results: 80% click-through rate for LLM-crafted phishing emails (cited from a Black Hat 2023 presentation, ref [12]; not the authors' own measurement)
1,265% increase in malicious phishing emails attributed to AI tools like ChatGPT (cited, ref [34]; not the authors' own measurement)
Financial services accounted for 24% of all U.S. data breaches; malware was the 3rd most common cyberattack vector in 2023 (cited from ITRC 2023 Data Breach Report, ref [26])
FraudGPT priced at ~$200/month for access (cited, ref [43])
OpenAI moderation endpoint filters 4 content categories: violence, self-harm, hate, sexual

AGI-timeline opinions (Table 2): Hinton '20 years or less'; OpenAI 'within the next ten years'; Cotra model >10% by 2036, ~50% by 2055, ~80% by 2100; 738 AI experts 50% chance by 2059; Metaculus median 2032
baselines_compared: Qualitative comparison of traditional (manual, time-consuming, skill-dependent, low variability) vs GenAI-assisted malware development (accelerated, higher variation, lowered skill barrier); no quantitative baseline benchmark
Comparison of blackhat/less-filtered LLMs (WormGPT [built on EleutherAI GPT-J 2021], FraudGPT, HackerGPT [GPT-3-based]) against guardrailed ChatGPT 3.5
